mappings.rationaleAI: Covers the later Renaissance in Italy and northern Europe, including cultural and intellectual life explicitly discussed in A.1.
Covers artistic achievements of the Italian and northern Renaissance, directly relevant to cultural developments in A.1.b.
Machiavelli and the principle of raison d'etat are explicitly cited in A.1.a as exemplifying Italian statecraft and political theory.
The emergence of modern science and technology in the 16th and 17th centuries is explicitly discussed in A.2.
The Protestant Reformation and its political and social consequences are a central theme of A.3.a.
The Catholic Reformation and Counter-Reformation are explicitly discussed in A.3.b as responses to Protestantism.
Luther, the Diet of Worms, and the Peasants' Revolt are explicitly covered in A.5.f.i on Germany and the Holy Roman Empire.
The emergence of Protestant churches across England, Scotland, France, Germany, and the Netherlands is a major theme throughout A.3-A.5.
Calvin's role in the Swiss Reformation and Calvinism's influence in Scotland, France, and the Netherlands are discussed in A.5.g.ii and A.5.d.vi.
Henry VII, Henry VIII, Edward VI, Mary I, and Elizabeth I are all covered in A.5.d.i-iii on the British Isles.
James I, Charles I, the English Civil War, Cromwell, the Commonwealth, and the Glorious Revolution are covered in A.5.d.iv-v and C.2.a.i.
The Hanoverian succession, Whig supremacy, ministries of Walpole and Pitt, and George III's early reign are covered in C.2.a.ii-iii.
The section concludes with the financial crisis, reform movement, and States General marking the beginning of the Revolution (C.2.b.vi).
Habsburg power in Spain, Austria, the Netherlands, and the Holy Roman Empire is a central theme from Charles V through Maria Theresa and Joseph II (A.5.b, C.2.c).
Germany and the Holy Roman Empire from c. 1500 to 1648, including the Reformation and Thirty Years' War, are covered in A.5.f.
European overseas expansion, establishment of colonial empires, and the impact of colonial expansion on Europe are major themes of B.
The age of the Enlightenment, its 17th-century origins, and the reform movement in France are explicitly discussed in C.3.
Voyages of discovery, advances in geographical knowledge, and establishment of colonial empires are explicitly discussed in B.1.
Advances in navigational instruments, ship design, and cartography enabling European overseas expansion are explicitly mentioned in B.1.a.
The English Civil War and 17th-century political upheaval provide the context for Hobbes's political philosophy, relevant to A.5.d.v.
The Glorious Revolution of 1688, limited monarchy, and growth of political parties are the context for Locke's philosophy, relevant to C.2.a.i.
The reform movement in France, nationalism, individualism, and the prelude to the Revolution connect to Rousseau's thought (C.2.b.vi).
Galileo's scientific contributions are central to the Scientific Revolution discussed in A.2.
Newton's work continues the Scientific Revolution into the late 17th century, relevant to A.2 and C.3.a.
Copernicus's heliocentric theory inaugurated the Scientific Revolution discussed in A.2.
Shakespeare's work is central to the cultural developments in Elizabethan and Jacobean England covered in A.5.d.iii-iv.
Persecution of Puritans under Charles I and Puritanism's role in the English Civil War and Cromwell's Commonwealth are covered in A.5.d.v.
Decline of feudalism, growth of commercial activity, joint-stock companies, and mercantilist policies are central economic themes of B.2-B.4.
The price revolution, growth of mercantilism, increase in world trade, and development of the slave trade are covered in B.4.
The development and importance of the slave trade in the context of European colonial expansion is explicitly discussed in B.4.a.v.
The conflict between nobility and bourgeoisie, the decline of feudalism, and aristocratic power across European states are discussed in B.2 and C.2.b.vi.
Ireland's subjugation by Henry VIII and Elizabeth I, the Irish revolt of 1641, and Cromwell's invasion are discussed in A.5.d.vii.
Scotland in the 16th-17th centuries, the Scottish Reformation, John Knox, and the 1707 union are covered in A.5.d.vi and C.2.a.v.
The establishment of the Church of England under Henry VIII and subsequent religious developments under Elizabeth I are discussed in A.5.d.ii-iii.
Origins of the Industrial Revolution and factory system, agricultural innovations, and pre-industrial manufacturing are discussed in B.3 and C.2.a.iv.
The influence of Methodism on 18th-century Britain is explicitly mentioned in C.2.a.iv.
International diplomacy and warfare from 1494 to 1763, the balance of power concept, and the alliance system are major themes of A.4 and C.1.
Spain's Golden Age cultural developments during the reigns of Philip II through Philip IV are discussed in A.5.b.i.
French cultural development in the 17th century and the intellectual life of the ancien regime are covered in C.2.b.iv-v.
The section is organized around dynastic states including the Habsburgs, Bourbons, Stuarts, Vasas, and Houses of Orange and Hanover.
The French Wars of Religion and 16th-century French cultural developments provide the context for Montaigne's essays (A.5.e.ii).
Thomas More was a key Tudor-era statesman relevant to the section's coverage of Henry VIII and the English Reformation (A.5.d.ii).
The ancien regime and the reform movement attacking political and social policies connect to Voltaire's role as a leading Enlightenment critic (C.2.b.v-vi).
Growth of mercantilism, commercial development, and origins of the Industrial Revolution provide the context for Adam Smith's economic thought (B.4, C.2.a.iv).
The development of German Idealism under Kant is discussed in the context of Frederick the Great's enlightened reform (C.2.d.ii).
The religiously divided 16th-17th century Europe and the Counter-Reformation created the context for early modern witch trials (A.3-A.4).
